_work.title: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
_work.creator: various
_work.rights: Public domain / CC0 (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The astrological reading of the sky in early-modern print and painting: the Great Comet of 1577 (the Tübingen / Daščický broadsheet over Prague, Tycho Brahe's diagram, the Istanbul sighting), the comet-form atlases of Hevelius (*Cometographia*, *Prodromus Cometicus*) and Lubieniecki (*Theatrum Cometicum*), the 1556 comet-and-earthquake over Constantinople, the 1680 comet over Rotterdam, the Kometenbuch comet-book, solar and lunar eclipses (Apian's volvelle, the Florentine Codex, the 1566 and 1832 eclipses), the Vädersolstavlan sun-dogs of 1535 Stockholm, Sebastian Brant's Ensisheim meteorite of 1492, and Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* — comets and eclipses read as warnings written in the heavens.

entries.title: The Great Comet of 1577 over Prague (Daščický broadsheet)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Jiří Jakubův Daščický (Daschitzský)
entries.summary:
The broadsheet *Von einem schrecklichen und wunderbarlichen Cometen* — Jiří Daščický's woodcut of the [[motif:great comet of 1577|Great Comet of 1577]] blazing over Prague on the Tuesday after Martinmas.

## Iconography
A huge yellow comet with a long curving tail arcs across the sky above the towers and bridges of Prague, the city laid out beneath while citizens point upward in alarm. Letterpress German text below narrates the apparition and its meaning as a fearful sign.

## Significance
The comet of November 1577 was the most observed of the century: Tycho Brahe used its parallax to prove it lay beyond the Moon, shattering the Aristotelian crystalline spheres. Yet for the broadsheet public it remained a [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] of war, plague, and divine wrath. Daščický's sheet, preserved in the Zurich Wickiana, is the iconic image of the comet read simultaneously as new science and old omen.

## For artists & game designers
A vast curving comet-tail sweeping over a detailed real skyline is the definitive 'the heavens have spoken' establishing shot.

citations.text: C. Doris Hellman, *The Comet of 1577: Its Place in the History of Astronomy* (1944).

entries.title: Tycho Brahe's Diagram of the Comet of 1577
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Tycho Brahe
entries.date: 1588 (engraving, from De mundi aetherei recentioribus phaenomenis)
entries.summary:
Tycho Brahe's diagram of the [[motif:great comet of 1577|Comet of 1577]] from *De mundi aetherei recentioribus phaenomenis liber secundus* (1588), plotting the comet's path among the planetary spheres.

## Iconography
A geometric cosmographic diagram centres the Earth, with the orbits of Sun, planets, and the comet traced as circles and a looping cometary track. The image is a schematic of the heavens rather than a landscape — the comet rendered as a measurable celestial body.

## Significance
Brahe's parallax measurements showed the comet lay far beyond the Moon, in the supposedly immutable aether, helping to dismantle the Aristotelian-Ptolemaic [[motif:celestial spheres|crystalline spheres]]. The same comet that broadsheets read as an omen of doom became, in Brahe's hands, a key instrument of the scientific revolution — astrology and the new astronomy meeting in a single apparition.

## For artists & game designers
The orbit-diagram look — nested circles with a looping comet track — signals 'learned astronomy' as opposed to the popular omen-broadsheet idiom.

citations.text: Tycho Brahe, *De mundi aetherei recentioribus phaenomenis* (Uraniborg, 1588).

entries.title: The Comet of 1577 over Istanbul
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Anonymous (Ottoman miniature)
entries.summary:
An Ottoman illustration of the [[motif:great comet of 1577|Comet of 1577]] (*kuyruklu yıldız*, 'tailed star') as seen from Istanbul, paired with a plan of the city.

## Iconography
A bright tailed star streaks above a schematic plan of Istanbul with its walls and waterways. The comet is shown as a fiery body trailing a long tail across the sky over the Ottoman capital.

## Significance
The 1577 comet was observed across the world, including by Taqī al-Dīn at the Istanbul Observatory, whose readings the Sultan's astrologers tied to political fortune. The observatory was demolished soon after, partly because the comet's promised omens were read as ill. The image shows that the astrological reading of comets was a shared Eurasian practice, not a European monopoly.

## For artists & game designers
Pairing a comet with a stylised city map shows an omen 'addressed to' a specific place — a device for tying a sign to a polity's fate.

citations.text: Avner Ben-Zaken, on Taqī al-Dīn and the Istanbul Observatory.

entries.title: Comet and Earthquake at Constantinople, 1556
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Herman Gall (broadsheet)
entries.summary:
A 1556 broadsheet by Herman Gall linking a [[motif:comet|comet]] to a destructive earthquake at Constantinople, presenting both as a single divine warning.

## Iconography
A tailed comet blazes over a walled city while buildings topple and the ground splits in an earthquake; figures flee amid collapsing towers. The print yokes the sky-sign and the earth-disaster in one composition.

## Significance
The comet of 1556 (sometimes linked to the abdication of Charles V) was read as a portent, and broadsheets eagerly coupled it with contemporary catastrophes such as earthquakes to demonstrate the heavens' predictive power. The pairing of [[motif:comet|comet]] above and [[motif:earthquake|earthquake]] below is a compressed argument that celestial signs cause or foretell terrestrial ruin — the core logic of judicial astrology in print.

## For artists & game designers
Stacking a sky-omen over an unfolding disaster in one frame is a strong 'cause and effect' layout for a prophecy panel.

citations.text: Roberta J. M. Olson & Jay M. Pasachoff, *Fire in the Sky: Comets and Meteors* (1998).

entries.title: The Great Comet of 1680 over Rotterdam (Verschuier)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Lieve Verschuier
entries.summary:
Lieve Verschuier's painting of the [[motif:great comet of 1680|Great Comet of 1680]] (*Staartster*, 'tail-star') hanging over Rotterdam as crowds gather to watch.

## Iconography
A brilliant vertical comet with a long upright tail dominates the twilight sky above the rooftops and harbour of Rotterdam, while throngs of townsfolk on the quay and rooftops gaze up, some kneeling. The painter records the collective experience of witnessing the apparition.

## Significance
The comet of 1680 (Kirch's Comet) was the first discovered by telescope and was tracked by Newton and Halley as a test case for cometary orbits. Yet Verschuier's canvas captures the popular reaction — awe, dread, and prayer — showing how a [[motif:celestial portent|celestial sign]] still gripped the public imagination even at the dawn of Newtonian astronomy.

## For artists & game designers
A crowd of upturned faces beneath a towering vertical comet conveys collective wonder more powerfully than the comet alone.

citations.text: Sara J. Schechner, *Comets, Popular Culture, and the Birth of Modern Cosmology* (1997).

entries.title: Notable Comets 1577–1652 (Hevelius, Cometographia)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Johannes Hevelius
entries.summary:
An engraved plate of notable [[motif:comet|comets]] of 1577–1652 from Johannes Hevelius' *Cometographia* (Danzig, 1668), the first comprehensive comet-atlas.

## Iconography
Rows of comets float against blank ground, each shown as a distinct head-and-tail form — straight, curved, fan-shaped, or beard-like — catalogued like specimens. The plate compares the varied shapes of historic comets side by side.

## Significance
Hevelius' *Cometographia* gathered every recorded comet into a systematic illustrated treatise, attempting (with a parabolic-path theory) to bring comets under natural law. The very act of cataloguing [[motif:cometary forms|comet forms]] marks the transition from comet-as-omen to comet-as-object, though the period's astrological dread still shadowed the enterprise. It set the template for the comet atlases of Lubieniecki and after.

## For artists & game designers
A grid of distinct comet silhouettes is a ready 'specimen chart' aesthetic — useful for a bestiary, spell-catalogue, or arcane reference page.

entries.title: Comet Plates (Hevelius, Prodromus Cometicus)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Johannes Hevelius
entries.summary:
Plates A and B from Johannes Hevelius' *Prodromus Cometicus* (1665), his observational study of the [[motif:comet of 1664|comet of 1664–65]].

## Iconography
Two engraved plates chart the comet's nightly positions and changing tail against star fields and reference grids, the comet head drawn at successive points along its track. The images are precise observational records rather than ominous broadsheets.

## Significance
The *Prodromus Cometicus* preceded Hevelius' larger *Cometographia* and documented the bright comet of 1664, one of a pair (1664 and 1665) that triggered a wave of [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] literature across Europe. Hevelius' careful plotting represents the astronomical response to the same comets that the broadsheets read as harbingers of plague — the Great Plague of London (1665) was widely blamed on them.

## For artists & game designers
A multi-position 'time-lapse' of a comet across a star chart shows motion and study — a scholarly counterpoint to the single dramatic omen image.

entries.title: Theatrum Cometicum Title Page (Lubieniecki)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Stanisław Lubieniecki (Stanislaus Lubienietzki)
entries.summary:
The engraved title page of Stanisław Lubieniecki's *Theatrum Cometicum* (1668), the monumental catalogue of all comets recorded in history.

## Iconography
An ornate allegorical frontispiece frames the title with figures of astronomy and history, comets streaking through the upper register. The architecture of the 'theatre' presents the comet-catalogue as a grand stage of cosmic history.

## Significance
Lubieniecki, a Socinian exile, compiled the *Theatrum Cometicum* as an exhaustive [[motif:comet catalogue|comet catalogue]] — over 400 comets back to the Flood — soliciting observations of the 1664–65 comets from astronomers across Europe by letter. The work straddles the era's tension: a quasi-scientific census of comets that still treats them within a framework of historical and theological meaning. Together with Hevelius it defines the great age of the printed comet-atlas.

## For artists & game designers
An allegorical 'theatre' frontispiece — figures, architecture, and comets framing a title — is a model for an in-world tome's grand opening page.

entries.title: Comet 'Rosa' (Kometenbuch, 1587)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Anonymous
entries.rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An illustration of the comet named *Rosa* ('the Rose') from a *Kometenbuch* (comet-book) of 1587, a typology of comet-shapes and their meanings.

## Iconography
A stylised comet is rendered as a glowing rose-like head trailing a tail, set against a plain ground with explanatory text. The comet is presented as a named type with its own distinctive form and astrological signification.

## Significance
The *Kometenbücher* were illustrated treatises, descended from the Arabic-Latin astrological tradition (via the *Liber de significatione cometarum* attributed to 'Ptolemy'/Ali ibn Ridwan), that classified comets into named kinds — *Rosa*, *Veru*, *Pertica*, *Miles* and others — each foretelling specific events such as the death of a king or the coming of war. The book makes explicit the [[motif:comet|comet]]'s role as a coded message whose shape determines its prophecy.

## For artists & game designers
A bestiary of named comet-types, each a distinct glowing form with a fixed prophecy, is a ready system for an in-world omen-classification mechanic.

citations.text: Roberta J. M. Olson & Jay M. Pasachoff, *Fire in the Sky* (1998).

entries.title: Cometographia Title Page (London, 1578)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Anonymous (London printer; Houghton STC 1416)
entries.summary:
The title page of an English *Cometographia* (London, 1578), a tract on the [[motif:great comet of 1577|comet of 1577]] and the meaning of comets.

## Iconography
A woodcut title page frames the text, typically with a blazing comet device, presenting the pamphlet as an authoritative discourse on the apparition's astrological and religious significance.

## Significance
The 1577 comet provoked a flood of pamphlets across Europe, England included, debating whether comets were natural exhalations or divine signs. English tracts of 1577–78 typically argued, with John of Damascus and the broadsheet tradition, that [[motif:comet|comets]] presaged the death of princes, dearth, and war — a conviction Shakespeare would echo. The printed *Cometographia* shows the comet-omen entering the vernacular English press.

## For artists & game designers
An ornamented title page announcing a treatise 'on the blazing star' models how an in-world book frames an omen as a subject of learned study.

citations.text: Sara J. Schechner, *Comets, Popular Culture, and the Birth of Modern Cosmology* (1997).

entries.title: Eclipse Volvelle (Apian, Astronomicum Caesareum)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Petrus Apianus (Peter Apian)
entries.summary:
An eclipse [[motif:volvelle|volvelle]] — a rotating paper instrument — from Petrus Apianus' luxurious *Astronomicum Caesareum* (1540).

## Iconography
Concentric rotating discs in jewel-like colour are calibrated to compute solar and lunar [[motif:eclipse|eclipses]]; a central disc shows the sun and moon, the dragon's head and tail (the lunar nodes) marked around the rim. The volvelle is both instrument and ornament.

## Significance
Apian's *Astronomicum Caesareum*, dedicated to Charles V, used ingenious movable volvelles to predict planetary positions and eclipses without calculation. Eclipses were prime astrological events — their timing and the zodiacal sign affected fed directly into nativities and mundane prognostication. The 'dragon' nodes that cause eclipses preserve the ancient myth of the eclipse-devouring serpent within a precision instrument.

## For artists & game designers
Layered rotating discs with celestial symbols are the archetypal 'magical computing device' — an analog astrolabe-machine for casting or predicting.

entries.title: Volvelle Plate f.18 (Apian, Astronomicum Caesareum)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Petrus Apianus (Peter Apian)
entries.summary:
A volvelle plate (folio 18) from Petrus Apianus' *Astronomicum Caesareum* (1540), a movable disc for computing celestial positions.

## Iconography
A large, vividly coloured [[motif:volvelle|volvelle]] of nested rotating discs is ringed by zodiacal signs and degree scales, with a thread and pointer for reading off positions. Coats of arms and ornament frame the instrument-page.

## Significance
Each of the *Astronomicum*'s volvelles models the motion of a planet or the eclipse cycle, turning the book into a working astronomical computer. Apian designed it for astrological practice at the imperial court — casting horoscopes and predicting the eclipses and conjunctions that mundane [[motif:prognostication|prognostication]] required. It is the most sumptuous printed book of the Renaissance and a high point of the marriage of art, instrument, and astrology.

## For artists & game designers
The heraldic-framed, multi-disc instrument page is a lavish template for a 'court astrologer's apparatus' prop.

entries.title: Lunar Eclipse of 1566 (Facial Chronicle)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Anonymous (Russian manuscript illuminators)
entries.summary:
A miniature recording the [[motif:lunar eclipse|lunar eclipse]] of 1566 from the *Illustrated (Facial) Chronicle of Ivan the Terrible*.

## Iconography
Within the chronicle's stacked-architecture style, figures observe a darkened or reddened moon above a city, the eclipse marked as a noted event in the historical narrative. The composition embeds the celestial sign into the flow of recorded history.

## Significance
The *Litsevoy letopisny svod* (Facial Chronicle), compiled in the 1560s–70s for Ivan IV, is one of the largest illustrated manuscripts ever made, with some 16,000 miniatures. Its inclusion of an [[motif:lunar eclipse|eclipse]] among wars and coronations shows the Muscovite court, like the Latin West, reading celestial events as part of providential history — eclipses as signs woven into the chronicle of the realm.

## For artists & game designers
An eclipse slotted between battle scenes in a chronicle shows how to treat a sky-omen as one entry in a running historical record.

citations.text: *Litsevoy letopisny svod* (Illustrated Chronicle of Ivan the Terrible), 1560s–70s.

entries.title: Lunar Eclipse (Florentine Codex, Book 7)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Bernardino de Sahagún and Nahua artists
entries.summary:
An illustration of a [[motif:lunar eclipse|lunar eclipse]] from Book 7 (on astronomy) of the *Florentine Codex* (c. 1577), Bernardino de Sahagún's encyclopaedia of Nahua knowledge.

## Iconography
The moon is shown eclipsed in the codex's hybrid Nahua-European style, set among the book's account of the sun, moon, stars, and the calendar. The image accompanies the Nahuatl text describing what the Mexica believed an eclipse portended.

## Significance
Book 7 of the *Florentine Codex* records that the Mexica feared a [[motif:lunar eclipse|lunar eclipse]] as a dangerous omen — pregnant women especially were thought at risk of monstrous birth — paralleling Old World eclipse-dread. Compiled by Sahagún with Nahua scholars and artists, it is a rare record of an indigenous astrology of the sky meeting European cosmology, both treating the eclipse as a fearful sign.

## For artists & game designers
A cross-cultural eclipse image shows that 'the moon is devoured / corrupted' reads as a portent across very different visual traditions.

citations.text: Bernardino de Sahagún, *Historia general de las cosas de Nueva España* (Florentine Codex), Book 7, c. 1577.

entries.title: An Eclipse over London, 1832 (British Museum)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Anonymous (British Museum print)
entries.summary:
A print depicting a [[motif:solar eclipse|solar eclipse]] as seen over London in 1832, held by the British Museum.

## Iconography
The darkened sun hangs over a recognisable London skyline as crowds in the streets observe the eclipse, some through smoked glass. The image treats the eclipse as a public urban spectacle rather than a dread omen.

## Significance
By 1832 the eclipse had largely passed from [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] to popular astronomy and entertainment, observed by crowds as a predictable wonder. The print marks the long arc from the eclipse-as-omen of the chronicles to the eclipse-as-public-science of the nineteenth century — even as almanac astrology and residual superstition lingered. It documents the secularisation of a once-fearful sign.

## For artists & game designers
The same eclipse motif, set amid a casually watching modern crowd, reads as 'spectacle' rather than 'omen' — a useful contrast to the dread broadsheets.

entries.title: Eclipse of the Sun with Unknown Comet, 1882
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: George Frederick Chambers
entries.summary:
An engraving of the total [[motif:solar eclipse|solar eclipse]] of 17 May 1882 with an unexpected [[motif:comet|comet]] visible beside the eclipsed sun, from G. F. Chambers' *The Story of the Comets*.

## Iconography
The black disc of the eclipsed sun blazes with its corona while a bright sungrazing comet streaks nearby — a rare conjunction of two celestial spectacles captured in one observation, set against the darkened sky.

## Significance
The 1882 eclipse coincided with the appearance of a comet (a Kreutz sungrazer, 'Tewfik') seen only during totality — a celebrated moment of observational astronomy. By this date the comet-and-eclipse were objects of scientific record, yet the image's drama recalls the older fusion of [[motif:comet|comet]] and [[motif:eclipse|eclipse]] as twinned celestial signs that earlier centuries would have read as a double portent.

## For artists & game designers
A comet beside a corona'd black sun is a striking 'two omens at once' image — maximal celestial drama in a single sky.

citations.text: George F. Chambers, *The Story of the Comets* (Oxford, 1909).

entries.title: Vädersolstavlan: The Sun-Dogs over Stockholm, 1535
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Urban målare (copy after); attributed
entries.summary:
The *Vädersolstavlan* ('Sun-Dog Painting'), depicting the [[motif:parhelion|parhelic]] halo display of mock-suns seen over Stockholm on 20 April 1535.

## Iconography
Above a meticulous early panorama of Stockholm, a complex of glowing rings, arcs, and [[motif:sun-dogs|mock-suns]] (*vädersolar*, 'weather-suns') fills the sky — multiple bright suns linked by haloes encircling the true sun. The city below is the oldest surviving image of Stockholm.

## Significance
The sun-dog display was read in the tense politics of the Swedish Reformation: opponents of King Gustav Vasa interpreted the apparition as a divine warning against him, prompting the king to commission a recording of the [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] partly to neutralise it. The surviving 1636 copy preserves the lost original. It is a paradigmatic case of an [[motif:atmospheric optics|atmospheric optical]] phenomenon read as a political omen.

## For artists & game designers
A full halo-complex (multiple suns, rings, tangent arcs) over a detailed cityscape is the richest possible 'wrong sky' set-piece.

citations.text: Sven Lidman & others on the *Vädersolstavlan*; Storkyrkan, Stockholm.

entries.title: The Ensisheim Meteorite of 1492 (Sebastian Brant broadsheet)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Sebastian Brant (text); Johannes Prüss (printer)
entries.rights: CC0 / Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
Sebastian Brant's broadsheet *Von dem Donnerstein gefallen zu Ensisheim* (1492), recording the fall of the [[motif:meteorite|Ensisheim meteorite]] as a heaven-sent sign.

## Iconography
A woodcut shows the great stone hurtling from the sky and striking a field outside the town of Ensisheim, with figures reacting to the *Donnerstein* ('thunderstone'). Letterpress verse by Brant interprets the event.

## Significance
The Ensisheim meteorite fell on 7 November 1492 — the oldest meteorite fall with a preserved date and the actual stone still surviving. Sebastian Brant, soon famous for the *Ship of Fools*, seized on it as a [[motif:celestial portent|portent]] favouring King Maximilian against the French, in one of the earliest single-leaf news broadsheets. The 'thunderstone' thus became propaganda, a [[motif:falling star|falling star]] read as divine endorsement.

## For artists & game designers
A stone trailing fire as it crashes into a field is the original 'star fell to earth' event — a portent that leaves a tangible relic behind.

citations.text: Ursula B. Marvin, on the Ensisheim meteorite of 1492.

entries.title: The Three Estates under the Stars (Lichtenberger, Prognosticatio, 1492)
entries.work: Celestial Portents: Comets, Eclipses & the Astrological Reading of the Sky
entries.creator: Johannes Lichtenberger
entries.summary:
A woodcut of the three estates beneath a planetary conjunction from Johannes Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* (Mainz, 1492), the most influential astrological prophecy of the age.

## Iconography
The [[motif:three estates|three estates]] — clergy, emperor, and commons — stand beneath the heavens where a [[motif:conjunction|planetary conjunction]] (Saturn and Jupiter) presides, the celestial alignment hanging over the social order as its determining sign.

## Significance
Lichtenberger's *Prognosticatio* (first issued 1488) built its prophecies on the great [[motif:conjunction|Saturn–Jupiter conjunction]] of 1484, foretelling upheaval, a false prophet, and reform; it ran through scores of editions and was reprinted by Luther himself with a preface. It is the archetype of the printed [[motif:prognostication|prognostication]] — astrology mobilised as political and religious prophecy, the conjunction read as the engine of history.

## For artists & game designers
Social ranks arrayed beneath a ruling planetary conjunction is a clean visual thesis that 'the stars govern the fate of the realm.'
